questions.question: The Planning Commission of India was replaced by:
questions.explanation: NITI Aayog (National Institution for Transforming India) replaced the Planning Commission in January 2015. It serves as a policy think tank.

questions.question: SEBI regulates which sector?
questions.explanation: SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India) is the regulatory body for securities markets in India, protecting investor interests.

questions.question: FDI stands for:
questions.explanation: FDI (Foreign Direct Investment) is investment made by a foreign entity in a business or production in another country, providing control or significant influence.

questions.question: The 'Gini Coefficient' measures:
options: Inequality of income distribution (0 = perfect equality, 1 = perfect inequality)
questions.explanation: Gini Coefficient (0 to 1 or 0 to 100) measures income inequality. 0 = perfect equality (everyone has same income); 1 = perfect inequality (one person has all income). India's Gini is approximately 0.35-0.38.

questions.question: The law of demand states that, all else equal:
options: When price of a good rises, quantity demanded falls (inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded)
questions.explanation: Law of Demand (Alfred Marshall): price and quantity demanded have an inverse relationship — as price rises, quantity demanded falls (consumers buy less; some switch to substitutes). Exceptions (Giffen goods, Veblen goods, necessities, wartime hoarding). Demand curve slopes downward.

questions.question: The law of supply states that, all else equal:
options: When price of a good rises, quantity supplied increases (direct/positive relationship between price and quantity supplied)
Supply is constant regardless of price
questions.explanation: Law of Supply: price and quantity supplied have a direct relationship — as price rises, producers are willing to supply more (higher profit incentive). Supply curve slopes upward. Exceptions: agricultural products (time lag), antiques/art (fixed supply). Market equilibrium: demand = supply.

questions.question: India's Planning Commission was replaced by NITI Aayog in:
options: January 2015 — NITI Aayog (National Institution for Transforming India) chaired by the Prime Minister, unlike Planning Commission
questions.explanation: NITI Aayog: established January 1, 2015, replacing the Planning Commission (est. 1950). NITI Aayog is a policy think tank (not a fund-allocating body like Planning Commission). Chaired by PM. CEO runs daily operations. Prepares national strategies and provides technical advice. Planning Commission's 5-year plans replaced by 3-year, 7-year strategies.

questions.question: India's Five Year Plans were a feature of which economic model?
options: Socialist/Mixed economy planning — Soviet-influenced centralized planning to guide economic development (1951-2017)
questions.explanation: India's Five-Year Plans (1951-2017, 12 plans total) were inspired by Soviet Union's central planning model. First Plan (1951-56): agriculture focus (Bhakra dam, community development). Second Plan (Nehru-Mahalanobis): heavy industry. 12th Plan ended 2017. NITI Aayog replaced with strategy documents.

questions.question: The White Revolution in India refers to:
options: Operation Flood (1970-1996) — massive increase in milk production; led by Dr. Verghese Kurien ('Milkman of India') through AMUL cooperative model
questions.explanation: White Revolution/Operation Flood: Dr. Verghese Kurien (NDDB — National Dairy Development Board) set up milk cooperatives (AMUL model) to eliminate middlemen, give fair price to farmers, and collect/process milk efficiently. India became world's largest milk producer. AMUL started in Anand, Gujarat, 1946.

questions.question: Public Distribution System (PDS) in India is meant to:
options: Distribute subsidized food grains (rice, wheat, sugar, kerosene) to Below Poverty Line (BPL) families through ration shops (Fair Price Shops)
questions.explanation: PDS: network of about 5.3 lakh Fair Price Shops (ration shops) distributing subsidized food. Managed jointly by central (Food Corporation of India procures and allocates) and state governments (distributes). NFSA 2013 (National Food Security Act) extended PDS to ~81 crore people (75% rural, 50% urban population).

questions.question: Sen's Capability Approach to development was proposed by:
options: Amartya Sen — Nobel Prize in Economics 1998; argues development should expand people's capabilities and freedoms, not just income
questions.explanation: Amartya Sen (Nobel 1998): capabilities approach — development as freedom/expansion of real opportunities. Focus on what people are actually able to do and be (capabilities), not just income. Influenced HDI design. Major works: 'Development as Freedom' (1999), 'The Idea of Justice'. Bengal famine research: famines caused by lack of entitlements, not just food scarcity.

questions.question: Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) is:
options: Investment by a foreign entity in a business in another country with significant control (typically 10%+ ownership) — in plant, machinery, or management control
questions.explanation: FDI: foreign entity invests in a domestic company (typically 10%+ equity = significant control). Brings: capital, technology, management expertise, global market access. FPI/FII: foreign portfolio investment (buying shares without control). India attracted $70 billion+ FDI in 2021-22. Top FDI sectors: services, computer, telecom, construction.

questions.question: SENSEX is the benchmark stock market index of:
options: Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE) — comprises 30 largest, most actively traded companies; launched January 1, 1986 (base year 1978-79 = 100)
questions.explanation: SENSEX (Sensitive Index): BSE's 30-stock index. NSE's benchmark: NIFTY 50 (50 stocks). BSE (1875): world's oldest stock exchange in Asia, at Dalal Street, Mumbai. NSE (1992): India's largest exchange by trading volume. SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India, 1992): regulator of securities market.

questions.question: The Kuznets Curve (Environmental Kuznets Curve) suggests:
options: Environmental degradation first increases then decreases as per capita income rises (inverted U-shape) — poor countries can't afford clean tech; rich countries can
Income inequality always rises with growth
questions.explanation: Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC): as income rises, pollution/environmental degradation first increases (industrialization), reaches a peak, then decreases (clean technology, service economy, environmental regulations). Named after Simon Kuznets. The original Kuznets Curve deals with income inequality (inverted U) as economic development progresses.

questions.question: India's first stock exchange was:
options: Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE, 1875) — established as 'The Native Share and Stock Brokers' Association' under a banyan tree on Dalal Street, Mumbai
questions.explanation: Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE, 1875): Asia's oldest stock exchange. Started informally in 1855 under a banyan tree, formalized as 'The Native Share and Stock Brokers' Association.' Moved to Dalal Street, Mumbai. India's second oldest exchange: Ahmedabad Stock Exchange (1894). Calcutta Stock Exchange: 1908. Madras Stock Exchange: 1937.

questions.question: The term 'multiplier effect' in economics means:
options: An initial change in spending leads to a larger final change in income — if multiplier = 2, then Rs 100 crore investment → Rs 200 crore income increase
questions.explanation: Keynesian Multiplier: k = 1/(1-MPC) = 1/MPS. Where MPC = Marginal Propensity to Consume (fraction of extra income spent). If MPC = 0.8, k = 1/(1-0.8) = 5. An injection of Rs 100 crore in government spending creates Rs 500 crore in total income (as each round of spending becomes income for someone else).

questions.question: India is classified as a:
options: Mixed economy — combines elements of market economy (private sector, price mechanism) and planned economy (government intervention, public sector)
questions.explanation: India's mixed economy (as in the Constitution's Directive Principles): public sector controls strategic industries (defense, rail, atomic energy), while private sector operates in most areas. Since 1991 reforms, India has moved significantly toward market economy with reduced government control.

questions.question: India's largest trading partner (exports + imports combined) in recent years is:
options: USA (largest export destination), China (largest import source) — China tops as overall trading partner by value
questions.explanation: India's top trading partners: USA is the largest export destination (~18% of India's exports). China is the largest source of imports (~15% of India's imports). As a bilateral trading partner (exports + imports combined), China and USA alternately top the list. UAE and Saudi Arabia are also major partners.

questions.question: Remittances from Non-Resident Indians (NRIs) contribute to India's:
options: Current Account (in Balance of Payments) — India is the world's largest remittance recipient (~$100 billion annually)
questions.explanation: NRI remittances: India consistently receives the highest remittances globally (~$100 billion in 2022-23). Recorded under 'transfers' in the Current Account of BoP. Major sources: USA, UAE, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, UK. They help finance India's current account deficit and support rural household incomes.

questions.question: The term 'Laissez-Faire' in economics means:
options: Let it be/do — minimal government intervention; markets self-regulate through price mechanism (Adam Smith's 'invisible hand')
questions.explanation: Laissez-faire (French: 'let do/let go'): economic philosophy advocating minimal government intervention. Markets, through price mechanism ('invisible hand' — Adam Smith, 'Wealth of Nations,' 1776), efficiently allocate resources. Associated with classical economics. Opposite: interventionism/planned economy.

questions.question: The Atal Innovation Mission (AIM) was established under:
options: NITI Aayog — to promote innovation and entrepreneurship across India through Atal Tinkering Labs (ATLs) in schools and Atal Incubation Centres (AICs)
questions.explanation: AIM (2016, under NITI Aayog): flagship initiative to promote innovation. Key programs: Atal Tinkering Labs (ATLs) — maker spaces in schools (Grades 6-12) with 3D printers, robotics kits, etc. Atal Incubation Centres (AICs): support startups. Atal Community Innovation Centres (ACICs): tier-2/3 cities and rural areas.

questions.question: Digital India mission aims to:
options: Transform India into a digitally empowered society — three pillars: digital infrastructure, digital services, digital literacy; launched 2015
questions.explanation: Digital India (2015): three pillars: (1) Digital Infrastructure (broadband highways, mobile connectivity, public internet access, eGovernance) (2) Digital Services (DigiLocker, e-Hospitals, e-Education, BHIM-UPI payments, Aadhaar) (3) Digital Literacy (PMGDISHA). India now has 900+ million internet users.

questions.question: The Swachh Bharat Abhiyan (Clean India Mission) was launched on:
options: October 2, 2014 (Gandhi Jayanti) — to achieve Open Defecation Free (ODF) India; 9 crore+ toilets built; PM Modi started by personally sweeping Valmiki colony, New Delhi
questions.explanation: Swachh Bharat Mission (Oct 2, 2014, Gandhi's 145th birthday): two sub-missions — SBM-Grameen (rural, MoDRWS) and SBM-Urban (MoHUA). Phase 1 (2014-19): ODF (Open Defecation Free) goal — 60 crore people stopped open defecation. Phase 2 (2020-25): ODF+, solid/liquid waste management, plastic waste management.

questions.question: The term 'circular economy' refers to:
options: An economic system that eliminates waste by reusing, repairing, refurbishing, and recycling materials — contrasts with linear economy (take-make-dispose)
Economic cycle of boom and bust
Circular flow of income in national accounting
questions.explanation: Circular Economy: regenerative system that keeps resources in use for as long as possible. Principles: reduce consumption, reuse products, recycle materials, recover energy. Contrasts with linear (take-make-waste) economy. UN SDGs include circular economy elements. EU's Circular Economy Action Plan (2020). Key to achieving net-zero and sustainable development.

questions.question: India's largest public sector undertaking (PSU) by revenue is:
options: Indian Oil Corporation (IOC) — petroleum refining and marketing; consistently India's largest company by revenue; followed by ONGC, BPCL
questions.explanation: Indian Oil Corporation (IOC): India's largest company by revenue (~Rs 8 lakh crore). It is a Fortune 500 company (ranked ~94th globally). Refines and markets petroleum products through its extensive pipeline and retail network (~30,000 outlets). Other large PSUs: ONGC (oil exploration), BPCL, HPCL, Coal India, SBI, LIC, NTPC.

questions.question: The Bharat Net project aims to:
options: Provide broadband connectivity to all Gram Panchayats (2.5 lakh panchayats) using OFC, radio, and satellite — run by BSNL/DOT; enable Digital India in rural areas
questions.explanation: BharatNet (2011, earlier NOFN — National Optical Fibre Network): world's largest rural broadband connectivity project. Connects all 2.5 lakh gram panchayats with optical fiber (OFC). Implemented by BSNL through state governments. Enables: e-governance, telemedicine, e-education, e-commerce in rural India. Essential backbone for Digital India.

questions.question: Long-run economic growth is determined by:
options: Improvements in productivity (technology, education, health, institutions) and accumulation of physical capital (Solow growth model)
questions.explanation: Long-run growth determinants (Robert Solow's model): capital accumulation (investment) and technological progress (total factor productivity). Endogenous growth theory (Paul Romer): knowledge, human capital, and innovation drive sustained growth. Key: education quality, R&D spending, institutions, rule of law, and infrastructure.

questions.question: Food Corporation of India (FCI) was established to:
options: Procure food grains from farmers at MSP, store, and distribute them through PDS — established 1965; HQ: New Delhi; India's largest food storage network
questions.explanation: FCI (1965, FCI Act 1964): functions: procurement of wheat and rice from farmers at MSP (mainly Punjab, Haryana, UP), building and managing strategic food reserves (buffer stocks), distributing to state governments for PDS at Central Issue Price (CIP). FCI's buffer stock of grain helps stabilize prices and ensure food security.

questions.question: The Smart Cities Mission in India aims to:
options: Develop 100 smart cities with smart infrastructure, IT connectivity, e-governance, clean energy, and quality of life improvements; cities selected through competition (challenges)
questions.explanation: Smart Cities Mission (June 2015): selected 100 cities through competition (Smart Cities Challenge). Each city gets Rs 500 crore from Centre + Rs 500 crore from State over 5 years. Focus: area-based development (retrofit, redevelop, greenfield) + pan-city smart solutions (ICT, e-governance). Covers: smart transport, water, energy, IT infrastructure.

questions.question: The Bharat Ratna, India's highest civilian award, was first given in:
options: 1954 — first recipients: C. Rajagopalachari (Rajaji), C.V. Raman, and Dr. S. Radhakrishnan; maximum 3 per year; given for exceptional service to nation
questions.explanation: Bharat Ratna (1954): India's highest civilian honor. First three recipients: C. Rajagopalachari (last Governor-General, Tamil Nadu), C.V. Raman (Nobel laureate physicist), and Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan (philosopher, later President). No monetary prize. Given for exceptional service in art, literature, science, and public service.

questions.question: The term 'trade surplus' means:
options: Exports > Imports (country earns more from exports than it spends on imports) — opposite of trade deficit
questions.explanation: Trade Balance = Exports - Imports. Trade Surplus: Exports > Imports (positive balance). Trade Deficit: Imports > Exports (negative balance). India consistently runs a trade deficit (imports more than exports). India's trade deficit ~$240 billion (2022-23). Offset partially by services surplus, remittances. China and Germany have large trade surpluses.

questions.question: The term 'natural monopoly' refers to:
options: Industry where one firm can produce output most efficiently (lowest cost) due to very high fixed costs — e.g., railways, electricity distribution, water supply, pipelines
questions.explanation: Natural Monopoly: occurs when average cost declines continuously with scale (economies of scale so large that one firm serves the whole market more efficiently than multiple competitors). Examples: electricity grid, railway tracks, water pipes, telecom infrastructure. Typically regulated by government (or government-owned) to prevent monopoly pricing.

questions.question: The National Investment and Infrastructure Fund (NIIF) was set up to:
options: Fund long-term infrastructure projects (roads, ports, airports, power, railways) through equity and quasi-equity — India's first sovereign wealth-type fund; established 2015
questions.explanation: NIIF (2015): India's quasi-sovereign wealth fund for infrastructure. 49% owned by Government of India; 51% by foreign/domestic institutional investors (Abu Dhabi Investment Authority, Singapore GIC, etc.). Three funds: Master Fund (greenfield/brownfield infra), Fund of Funds, Strategic Opportunities Fund. Total committed capital: ~$4.5 billion.

questions.question: The phenomenon of 'Dutch Disease' refers to:
options: Boom in one sector (e.g., natural resource discovery) causing currency appreciation and decline in other export sectors (especially manufacturing) — named after Netherlands' natural gas boom in 1960s
questions.explanation: Dutch Disease: named after Netherlands' North Sea gas discovery (1959). Large foreign currency inflows from resource boom → currency appreciates → manufacturing becomes uncompetitive → deindustrialization. Examples: Nigeria (oil), Venezuela (oil). Prevention: sovereign wealth funds (Norway), diversification strategies.

questions.question: The Bharat Broadband Network Limited (BBNL) was merged with BSNL to implement:
options: BharatNet project — providing optical fiber broadband to all gram panchayats; BBNL was Special Purpose Vehicle set up 2012; merged with BSNL 2023
questions.explanation: BBNL (Bharat Broadband Network Limited, 2012): Special Purpose Vehicle to implement BharatNet (initially NOFN). In 2023, government merged BBNL with BSNL to streamline the BharatNet project and leverage BSNL's existing infrastructure, workforce, and right-of-way permissions for faster rural broadband rollout.

questions.question: The Agricultural and Processed Food Products Export Development Authority (APEDA) is under:
options: Ministry of Commerce and Industry — promotes export of fresh fruits/vegetables, processed foods, meat, cereals, and other agricultural products; established 1986
questions.explanation: APEDA (1986, APEDA Act 1985, HQ New Delhi): statutory body under Commerce Ministry. Promotes and regulates export of: scheduled products (fresh fruits/vegetables, flower, processed foods, meat, poultry, dairy, cereals, etc.). Assigns registration certificate to exporters; sets quality standards; develops packaging, marketing, and infrastructure.

questions.question: Tamil Nadu's economy is ranked among Indian states by GSDP (Gross State Domestic Product) as:
options: 2nd or 3rd largest economy in India (after Maharashtra and sometimes tied with Karnataka/UP) — known for manufacturing, automobiles, IT, and textiles
questions.explanation: Tamil Nadu: GSDP ~Rs 25+ lakh crore (2023-24), consistently among top 3 states (Maharashtra is typically 1st). TN contributes significantly to: auto manufacturing (Chennai = Detroit of India), textiles (Tirupur knit capital), leather (Vellore), IT (Chennai, Coimbatore), and tourism.

questions.question: Tirupur city in Tamil Nadu is known as:
options: Knit City or Dollar City — world's largest knitwear exporting hub; exports Rs 30,000+ crore annually; ~90% of India's cotton knitwear exports
questions.explanation: Tirupur (Tiruppur): world's largest exporter of knitted cotton garments. About 6,000 garment units, ~600,000 workers. Export markets: UK, USA, EU, Australia. Exports ~$4 billion annually. 'Dollar City' because of high foreign exchange earnings. Also known as 'Banian City' for banian/vest production.

questions.question: The Chennai-Bengaluru Industrial Corridor (CBIC) is aimed at:
options: Creating integrated manufacturing clusters along the Chennai-Bengaluru National Highway (NH-48) in TN and Karnataka — focus: electronics, automobiles, IT hardware
questions.explanation: Chennai-Bengaluru Industrial Corridor (CBIC): planned industrial corridor along NH-48 through Tamil Nadu (Hosur, Krishnagiri, Ranipet) and Karnataka. Focus: electronics manufacturing, automobiles, IT hardware, textiles. Part of India's industrial corridor development program along with DMIC (Delhi-Mumbai), AKIC (Amritsar-Kolkata).

questions.question: Tamil Nadu's major revenue earner from tourism includes:
options: Mahabalipuram (UNESCO), Madurai temples, Ooty hill station, Rameshwaram, Kancheepuram, Marina Beach — TN is one of India's most visited states
questions.explanation: Tamil Nadu tourism (3rd most visited state by foreign tourists in India): UNESCO World Heritage Sites (Mahabalipuram, Brihadeeswarar Temple Thanjavur, Airavatesvara Temple Darasuram, Gangaikonda Cholapuram), religious sites (Rameshwaram, Madurai Meenakshi, Tiruchendur), hill stations (Ooty, Kodaikanal, Yercaud).

questions.question: The Neyveli Lignite Corporation (NLC India) is located in:
options: Neyveli, Cuddalore district, Tamil Nadu — mines lignite (brown coal) and operates thermal power plants; also has solar power plants; public sector under Centre
questions.explanation: NLC India (formerly Neyveli Lignite Corporation): Central PSU in Neyveli, Cuddalore district. World's largest open-cast lignite mine. Mines lignite → burns in thermal plants → generates electricity (mainly supplied to Tamil Nadu and southern states). Also has major solar power capacity. Established 1956.

questions.question: The economic concept 'invisible hand' was given by:
options: Adam Smith (in 'Wealth of Nations,' 1776) — the idea that individuals pursuing self-interest in free markets unintentionally promote public good through competition
questions.explanation: Adam Smith's 'invisible hand': when individuals act in their own self-interest (profit motive) in competitive markets, they are guided as if by an 'invisible hand' to produce goods/services society wants at efficient prices. Basis of free-market capitalism. Wealth of Nations (1776) also argued for division of labor, free trade, and against mercantilism.

questions.question: The 'demographic transition' model describes:
options: Four stages of population change from high birth/death rates (pre-industrial) to low birth/death rates (post-industrial) — most developing countries are in stage 2-3 (high birth, declining death rates)
questions.explanation: Demographic Transition Model (Warren Thompson, 1929): Stage 1: high birth + high death rates (pre-industrial, stable population). Stage 2: high birth + declining death (rapid population growth — India in this stage since 1950s). Stage 3: declining birth + low death (slowing growth). Stage 4: low birth + low death (stable, developed countries).

questions.question: The 'multiplier-accelerator interaction' in Keynesian economics explains:
options: Business cycles: initial investment increases income (multiplier effect) → increased income raises demand for capital goods (accelerator effect) → further investment → cycles of expansion and contraction
questions.explanation: Keynesian business cycle theory: Multiplier (ΔY = k × ΔI) + Accelerator (ΔI = v × ΔY): initial autonomous investment → income multiplied by k → higher income accelerates further investment → boom (expansion). When income growth slows, acceleration reverses, investment falls, income multiplier works downward → recession. Paul Samuelson formalized this interaction.

questions.question: The 'principal-agent problem' in economics occurs when:
options: Agent (employee/manager) may not act in the best interests of principal (owner/employer) due to information asymmetry and different incentives — leads to moral hazard and adverse selection
Government is the agent for citizens
Central bank is agent for government
Banks are agents for depositors only
questions.explanation: Principal-Agent Problem: principal (owner, shareholder) hires agent (manager, employee) but cannot perfectly monitor agent's actions. Agent may shirk, take excessive risks, or pursue own interests at principal's expense. Solutions: incentive contracts (bonuses, stock options), monitoring, reputation effects. Related: corporate governance, insurance (insurer-insured), doctor-patient.

questions.question: The National Payments Corporation of India (NPCI) is responsible for:
options: Operating India's retail payment systems — UPI, RuPay (card network), IMPS, NACH, Bharat Bill Payment System (BBPS), FASTag (toll collection); set up by RBI and IBA
questions.explanation: NPCI (2008): non-profit umbrella organization for retail payment systems. Products: RuPay (domestic card network competing with Visa/Mastercard), IMPS (Immediate Payment Service, 24×7), UPI (Unified Payments Interface), NACH (National Automated Clearing House for bulk transactions), BBPS (Bharat Bill Payment System), FASTag (electronic toll collection).

questions.question: The 'Big Push' theory of economic development was proposed by:
options: Paul Rosenstein-Rodan (1943) — argues developing countries need massive coordinated investment across sectors simultaneously to escape poverty trap
questions.explanation: Big Push Theory (Rosenstein-Rodan, 1943): developing countries face coordination failure — no single entrepreneur invests because market too small, but if all invest simultaneously, market becomes viable. Solution: coordinated, large-scale investment across sectors simultaneously. Influenced India's Five Year Plans (simultaneous industrialization across sectors).

questions.question: India's Sovereign Credit Rating is assessed by:
options: International credit rating agencies: Moody's, S&P (Standard & Poor's), Fitch — India's rating is 'Baa3' (Moody's, lowest investment grade) / 'BBB-' (S&P/Fitch)
questions.explanation: Sovereign credit ratings measure a country's ability to repay debt. India: Moody's Baa3 (lowest investment grade — BBB equivalent); S&P BBB-; Fitch BBB- (all at lowest investment grade). Higher rating → lower borrowing cost for government and companies. India's stable outlook means no immediate upgrade/downgrade expected.

questions.question: An 'inclusive growth' strategy targets:
options: Economic growth that benefits all sections of society — particularly poor, marginalized, women, tribals, minorities — through social protection, education, health, employment generation
questions.explanation: Inclusive Growth: broad-based growth where benefits reach the bottom of the pyramid. India's Planning Commission and NITI Aayog emphasize inclusive growth. Indicators: HDI improvement, poverty reduction, gender parity, social mobility, access to basic services. Related: sustainable development goals (SDGs) — 'leaving no one behind.'

questions.question: The 'Marshall Plan' was:
options: US aid program to rebuild Western European economies after World War II (1948-1952) — $13 billion in grants/loans; rebuilt Germany, France, Italy etc.; named after Secretary of State George Marshall
questions.explanation: Marshall Plan (European Recovery Program, 1948-52): US provided $13 billion (equivalent to ~$160 billion today) in economic aid to rebuild Western Europe after WWII. Helped restore industrial production, trade, and prevent Soviet influence from spreading to economically devastated Europe. George Marshall (US Secretary of State) proposed it; won Nobel Peace Prize 1953.

questions.question: The G20 group comprises:
options: 19 major economies plus European Union (20 members) — represents ~85% of world GDP, ~75% of international trade, ~67% of world population; annual summits of leaders
questions.explanation: G20: Argentina, Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Russia, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, South Korea, Turkey, UK, USA + EU. India held G20 presidency 2023 (September summit in New Delhi). G7 (wealthy democracies) is a subset. G20 deals with global financial stability, trade, climate, development.

questions.question: SAARC (South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation) was established in:
options: 1985 (Dhaka Declaration) — 8 members: India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nepal, Bhutan, Sri Lanka, Maldives, Afghanistan; HQ Kathmandu; largely ineffective due to India-Pakistan tensions
questions.explanation: SAARC (1985): 8 South Asian nations. Charter signed December 8, 1985 (South Asian Day). HQ: Kathmandu, Nepal. SAARC Development Fund. South Asian Free Trade Area (SAFTA, 2004): very limited success due to non-tariff barriers and India-Pakistan political tensions. BIMSTEC is seen as alternative (Bay of Bengal Initiative).

questions.question: India's Merchandise Exports have the following major categories:
options: Engineering goods (~26%), petroleum products (~17%), gems & jewellery (~12%), chemicals (~12%), textiles & apparel (~10%) are top categories
questions.explanation: India's top merchandise export categories: Engineering goods (machinery, electronics, metal products), Petroleum products (refined petroleum), Gems and Jewellery (cut diamonds, gold jewellery), Chemicals and pharmaceuticals, Textiles and apparel. Services exports are separate and even larger: IT/ITES (~$250 billion). Total merchandise exports: ~$450 billion; services: ~$250+ billion.

questions.question: Credit Default Swaps (CDS) are financial instruments that:
options: Transfer credit risk of a bond/loan from buyer to seller for a premium — buyer pays periodic fee; if default occurs, seller compensates buyer; central to 2008 global financial crisis
Are a type of government bond
Are used only for currency trading
questions.explanation: CDS (Credit Default Swap): a derivative that works like insurance on a bond. If the reference entity (company/country) defaults, the CDS seller pays the buyer the face value. AIG's massive CDS exposure caused its near-collapse in 2008 (US government bailout: $182 billion). Warren Buffett called derivatives 'weapons of mass financial destruction.'

questions.question: The Participatory Notes (P-Notes) are:
options: Instruments issued by registered FPIs (Foreign Portfolio Investors) to overseas investors who want to invest in Indian markets without being registered with SEBI — controversial (anonymous ownership)
questions.explanation: Participatory Notes (P-Notes or ODIs — Offshore Derivative Instruments): FPIs registered with SEBI issue P-Notes to foreign investors (who may not want direct SEBI registration). Allows anonymous investment in Indian markets. SEBI has tightened P-Note regulations (2017): disclosure of beneficial owner required. P-Note exposure reduced from ~50% to ~3-5% of total FPI holdings.

questions.question: 'Disinvestment' in Indian public sector means:
options: Government selling its equity stake in PSUs (Public Sector Undertakings) — partial disinvestment: retains majority; strategic disinvestment: transfers management control; 'privatization' used when full control is transferred
questions.explanation: Disinvestment: government sells equity shares in PSUs to raise revenue and improve PSU efficiency. Types: Minority disinvestment (retains >51%, no control change), Majority disinvestment (retains <51%), Strategic Disinvestment (control transferred — like Air India sale to Tata 2022). DIPAM (Dept of Investment and Public Asset Management) manages disinvestment. Annual target: Rs 50,000-60,000 crore.

questions.question: The Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP) under IBC 2016 must be completed in:
options: 180 days + 90-day extension (maximum 330 days including litigation) — beyond which liquidation is initiated automatically unless 90% creditors agree to further extension
questions.explanation: CIRP (Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process) under IBC: 180 days from admission. Can extend by 90 days (NCLT approval). If no resolution plan approved within 270 days (330 with litigation-excluded period) → liquidation. NCLT (National Company Law Tribunal) adjudicates IBC cases. Success stories: Essar Steel, Bhushan Steel resolved through IBC (ArcelorMittal, Tata Steel as acquirers).

questions.question: The 'Dutch Flower Auction' pricing mechanism is used for:
options: Price starting high and descending until a buyer accepts — also called 'descending auction' or 'clock auction'; used for flowers, fish, tobacco, and some treasury securities
Price starting low and going up (English auction)
questions.explanation: Dutch (Descending) Auction: auctioneer starts at high price, lowers until a buyer bids. First bidder who accepts becomes the buyer. Advantage: fast (one round). Used in: Dutch flower market (hence name), fish markets, US Treasury auctions (actually uniform price), Google's IPO. Contrasts with English (ascending) auction where price rises until one bidder remains.

questions.question: The 'Production Possibility Frontier' (PPF) concept illustrates:
options: Maximum combinations of two goods that an economy can produce given limited resources — points on the curve = efficient; inside = inefficient; outside = currently unattainable (requires growth)
questions.explanation: PPF (Production Possibility Curve): illustrates scarcity and trade-offs. Economy with fixed resources and technology can produce combinations along the PPF frontier. Opportunity cost: producing more of one good requires producing less of another (resources are transferred). Economic growth (more resources, better technology) shifts PPF outward. Key concept in macroeconomics.

questions.question: National Commodity and Derivatives Exchange (NCDEX) and MCX are:
options: Commodity exchanges for trading agricultural commodities, metals, and energy futures — MCX: metals, energy; NCDEX: agricultural commodities; regulated by SEBI (since 2015, earlier FMC)
questions.explanation: Commodity exchanges: MCX (Multi Commodity Exchange, 2003): gold, silver, crude oil, base metals, natural gas. NCDEX (2003): agricultural commodities — chana (chickpea), soybean, guar, turmeric, pepper. FMC (Forward Markets Commission) regulated; merged with SEBI in 2015. Commodity futures allow farmers and traders to hedge price risk.

questions.question: The 'Economic corridor' concept in India refers to:
options: Integrated industrial and logistics infrastructure along transportation routes — Delhi-Mumbai Industrial Corridor (DMIC), Chennai-Bengaluru (CBIC), Amritsar-Kolkata (AKIC), Bangalore-Mumbai (BMIC)
questions.explanation: Industrial Corridors: Delhi-Mumbai IC (DMIC, flagship: 1483 km along DFC — Dedicated Freight Corridor, 24 manufacturing nodes, $90 billion investment plan). Chennai-Bengaluru IC (CBIC). Amritsar-Kolkata IC (AKIC). Hyderabad-Nagpur, Hyderabad-Warangal IC. Funded by: National Industrial Corridor Development Corporation (NICDC).

questions.question: The 'brain drain' problem in developing economies like India refers to:
options: Emigration of highly skilled professionals (doctors, engineers, scientists, IT workers) to developed countries seeking higher wages and better opportunities — India loses human capital
Internal migration from villages to cities (rural-urban migration)
questions.explanation: Brain Drain: emigration of talented, educated citizens to USA, UK, Canada, Australia etc. India loses significant numbers of IIT/IIM graduates, doctors, and scientists. Counter-argument: remittances ($100B), diaspora investment, knowledge transfer. Brain Drain vs Brain Gain vs Brain Circulation. Recently: 'reverse brain drain' as some Indian-origin professionals return.

questions.question: The concept of 'utility' in economics refers to:
options: Satisfaction or pleasure derived from consuming a good or service — basis of consumer behavior theory; cannot be directly measured
questions.explanation: Utility: subjective satisfaction from consuming goods/services. Cardinal utility: measurable in 'utils' (Bentham, Marshall). Ordinal utility: only rank preference (Hicks). Marginal utility: satisfaction from consuming one more unit. Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility: as more units consumed, additional satisfaction decreases.

questions.question: A 'monopoly' market exists when:
options: Only one seller in the market with no close substitutes — seller has maximum market power to set prices (price maker); examples: PSPCL (Punjab electricity), natural monopolies
questions.explanation: Monopoly: single seller, many buyers, no close substitutes, high barriers to entry. Monopolist is a price-maker (sets price or quantity, not both simultaneously). Types: natural monopoly (railways, utilities), legal monopoly (patents, copyrights), geographic monopoly. Profits maximized at MR = MC. DPL in distribution, Delhi Metro in metro system are examples.

questions.question: 'Oligopoly' is a market structure characterized by:
options: Few large sellers who are mutually interdependent — each firm's decision (price, output) affects and is affected by others; examples: telecom (Jio, Airtel, Vi), aviation, automobile industry
questions.explanation: Oligopoly: few dominant firms, high barriers to entry, mutual interdependence (each firm watches rivals). Models: Cournot (quantity competition), Bertrand (price competition), Stackelberg (leader-follower), Kinked Demand Curve. Tendency toward price rigidity and collusion (cartel). Indian examples: telecom, cement, automobiles, FMCG.

questions.question: 'Perfect competition' market characteristics include:
options: Many buyers and sellers, homogeneous product, free entry/exit, perfect information, no firm has market power — all firms are price takers; examples: agricultural commodity markets
questions.explanation: Perfect Competition: many small buyers and sellers, identical/homogeneous product (wheat, sugar), perfect information, free entry/exit, no transport costs. Each firm is a price-taker (accepts market price). Long-run: zero economic profit (price = minimum ATC). Closest real-world examples: agricultural markets, foreign exchange, some financial markets.

questions.question: Income elasticity of demand measures:
options: % change in quantity demanded / % change in income — Normal goods: positive (>0); Luxury goods: >1; Inferior goods: negative (quantity demanded falls as income rises)
questions.explanation: Income Elasticity (YED) = %ΔQd / %ΔIncome. Normal goods: YED > 0 (demand rises with income). Luxury goods: YED > 1 (demand rises faster than income — foreign travel, jewelry). Inferior goods: YED < 0 (demand falls as income rises — coarse grains, cheap substitutes). Engel's Law: as income rises, food share of spending falls.

questions.question: Cross elasticity of demand measures:
options: % change in demand for good A when price of good B changes — positive = substitutes (A and B); negative = complements (used together like car and petrol); zero = unrelated goods
questions.explanation: Cross Price Elasticity (XED) = %ΔQd(A) / %ΔP(B). Substitutes (positive XED): if price of Pepsi rises → demand for Coke increases. Complements (negative XED): if price of cars rises → demand for petrol falls. Unrelated goods (XED ≈ 0). Useful for antitrust analysis: high positive XED means products are in same market.

questions.question: The law of diminishing returns (short run) states:
options: As more units of a variable input (labor) are added to fixed inputs (capital/land), eventually marginal product of the variable input will decline
All factors of production decline over time
questions.explanation: Law of Diminishing Marginal Returns: in the short run (when at least one input is fixed), adding more of a variable input → initially rising, then constant, then falling marginal product. Example: more workers on a fixed plot of land → initially productive, but eventually crowded/counterproductive. Applies only when at least one input is fixed (short run).

questions.question: India's largest agricultural export in recent years has been:
options: Marine products (seafood — shrimps/prawns: ~70% of marine exports) and rice (both non-basmati and basmati); also engineering goods, petroleum products compete for top position
questions.explanation: India's top agricultural exports: Non-basmati rice (became no. 1 rice exporter globally), Marine products (mainly vannamei shrimps to USA, Japan, China), Basmati rice, Spices, Sugar, Cotton, Fresh fruits/vegetables. APEDA oversees agricultural exports. India's total agri exports: ~$50 billion (2022-23).

questions.question: India's National Monetisation Pipeline targets which assets?
options: Brownfield public infrastructure assets (already built) — NHAI roads, railway stations/tracks/freight terminals, airports (AAI), telecom towers (BSNL), power transmission lines (PGCIL), gas pipelines, sports stadiums
questions.explanation: NMP (2021-25, Rs 6 lakh crore target): monetize operational public infrastructure by giving long-term lease/right of use to private sector. Asset categories: Roads (NHAI: Rs 1.6 lakh cr), Railways (Rs 1.5 lakh cr — freight terminals, stations, hills railways), Power (PGCIL, NTPC: Rs 0.85 lakh cr), Telecom (BSNL, BBNL), Oil & Gas (GAIL, IOCL), Airports, Ports, Stadiums, Warehouses.

questions.question: The National Pension System (NPS) investment options include:
options: Active Choice (choose allocation across Equity E, Corporate Bonds C, Government Securities G up to 75% in E) and Auto Choice (lifecycle fund that automatically reduces equity as age increases)
questions.explanation: NPS Investment Options: (1) Active Choice: subscriber allocates among E (Equity: up to 75% for private sector), C (Corporate Bonds), G (Govt Securities), A (Alternative Assets: up to 5%). (2) Auto Choice/Lifecycle Fund: equity allocation high when young (75%), automatically decreases with age. Three auto lifecycle funds: LC75 (aggressive), LC50 (moderate), LC25 (conservative).

questions.question: India's 'National Mineral Policy 2019' was framed to:
options: Promote sustainable mining, attract private investment, expedite approvals, and ensure local area development fund for mining areas — replaces 2008 policy
questions.explanation: National Mineral Policy 2019: encourages private sector in mining (100% FDI allowed in mining). Promotes sustainable development (District Mineral Foundation for local communities), ease of doing business (online approvals, single window clearance), and mining transparency. MMDR Act 1957 amendments (2021): auction-based allotment, composite licence.

questions.question: India's Startup India initiative defines a 'startup' as an entity that is:
options: Incorporated < 10 years ago, annual turnover < Rs 100 crore, working toward innovation/improvement of products/services, and certified by DPIIT (not formed by splitting up a company)
questions.explanation: DPIIT Startup definition (2019): incorporated as private limited company, LLP, or registered partnership; < 10 years old; annual turnover < Rs 100 crore in any FY; working on innovation/improvement of products, services, or processes (not mere improvement in existing business). Must be DPIIT-recognized. India: 100,000+ DPIIT-recognized startups (3rd largest globally).

questions.question: India's 'demographic dividend' window is approximately:
options: 2020-2040 (or 2020-2055 per some estimates) — when working-age population (15-64) is largest relative to dependents; requires investments in education, health, and job creation to capitalize
questions.explanation: India's Demographic Dividend: median age ~28 (young population). Working-age population peak relative to dependents: approximately 2020s-2040s. To benefit: need quality education and skills (avoid 'demographic disaster' of unemployable youth), jobs for 10 million youth entering workforce annually, women workforce participation (only ~25% currently), and urban infrastructure.

questions.question: The Wholesale Price Index (WPI) in India has a base year of:
options: 2011-12 (revised from 2004-05 in 2017) — covers 697 commodities; primary articles 22.6%, fuel 13.2%, manufactured products 64.2%; monthly release with 14-day lag
questions.explanation: WPI base year revised to 2011-12 (released in May 2017) from 2004-05. Coverage: 697 commodities (vs 676 earlier). Weight: Primary Articles 22.62% (food: 15.26%, non-food: 4.29%, minerals: 3.07%), Fuel & Power 13.15%, Manufactured Products 64.23%. Released by Office of Economic Adviser (Ministry of Commerce). 14-day lag (faster than CPI's 12-day lag).

questions.question: The concept of 'social capital' in economics (Robert Putnam) refers to:
options: Networks, norms, trust, and reciprocity within communities that facilitate collective action and economic coordination — high social capital reduces transaction costs and improves governance
questions.explanation: Social Capital (Robert Putnam, 'Making Democracy Work,' 1993): norms of trust, networks of civic engagement (cooperatives, community associations), and reciprocity within communities. High social capital (northern Italy, Scandinavia): better economic performance, better governance. Low social capital: high corruption, low trust, higher transaction costs. MGNREGA SHGs and cooperatives build social capital.

questions.question: India's 'growth triangle' or dynamic regions of economic growth include:
options: National Capital Region (NCR): Delhi-Gurgaon-Faridabad; Silicon Plateau: Bengaluru-Pune; Manufacturing Belt: Chennai-Hosur; Western Industrial: Mumbai-Pune-Surat-Ahmedabad
questions.explanation: India's economic growth clusters: NCR (financial/IT/manufacturing hub), Mumbai Metropolitan Region (financial capital, Bollywood, pharmaceuticals), Bengaluru (IT hub), Pune-Nashik-Aurangabad (auto/IT/pharma), Hyderabad (IT/pharma, 'Cyberabad'), Chennai-Coimbatore-Hosur (auto/IT/textiles), Surat-Ahmedabad-Vadodara (textiles/petrochemicals/diamonds).

questions.question: The concept of 'crowdfunding' as a source of startup finance means:
options: Raising small amounts of money from many people (usually via internet platforms) — rewards-based (Kickstarter), equity-based (SEBI regulated in India since 2021), or donation-based
questions.explanation: Crowdfunding: raising capital from many small contributors online. Types: Reward-based (backers get product/service — Kickstarter, Indiegogo), Equity-based (backers get shares — regulated by SEBI in India as 'crowd sourcing/alternative investment'), Debt-based (peer-to-peer lending), Donation-based (charity). SEBI issued framework for Social Stock Exchanges (2022) for nonprofit crowdfunding.

questions.question: The Economic Survey 2016-17 coined the term 'Universal Basic Income' (UBI) for India as:
options: A concept where every citizen receives a fixed cash transfer regardless of income or employment status — would replace multiple targeted subsidies with a single universal transfer
questions.explanation: UBI (discussed in India's Economic Survey 2016-17, Arvind Subramanian): fixed cash income to every citizen, no means-testing. Pros: eliminates complex targeting, empowers recipients, reduces middlemen/leakage. Cons: costly, may reduce work incentive, may not replace in-kind benefits (food, health). India's closest: PM-KISAN (Rs 6,000/year to farmers). Not yet adopted fully.

questions.question: The 'Golden Quadrilateral' is:
options: National Highway network connecting India's 4 major metros: Delhi-Mumbai-Chennai-Kolkata — total 5,846 km; India's first major highway development project (NHDP); started under PM Vajpayee 2001
questions.explanation: Golden Quadrilateral (NHDP Phase 1): 4-6 lane highways connecting Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, and Kolkata. Total: 5,846 km. Developed by NHAI under National Highway Development Project (Phase 1, 2001). Substantially completed by 2012. Connected India's largest economic centers; greatly reduced transport time. Also 'North-South and East-West Corridor' connecting Srinagar-Kanyakumari and Porbandar-Silchar.

questions.question: Privatization of Air India was completed in:
options: January 2022 — Tata Sons (through Talace Pvt Ltd) acquired Air India for Rs 18,000 crore; ending government ownership since 1953 nationalization; Air India's homecoming to Tatas (original founder JRD Tata)
questions.explanation: Air India disinvestment: launched 2020, completed January 27, 2022. Tata Sons won the bid with enterprise value of Rs 18,000 crore (Rs 2,700 crore cash + Rs 15,300 crore debt assumption). Air India was founded by J.R.D. Tata (1932 as Tata Airlines) → nationalized 1953 → returned to Tatas after 69 years. Tata also owns Vistara, Air Asia India (merged into Air India).

questions.question: The 'Make in India' lion symbol is a:
options: Walking lion made of cogwheels (gear wheels) — symbolizing manufacturing and movement; designed by Wieden+Kennedy London; launched September 25, 2014 in Delhi
questions.explanation: Make in India logo: stylized lion (inspired by Ashoka Chakra wheel lion) with body composed of cogwheels/gears — symbolizing manufacturing, industrial growth, and India moving forward. Lion represents strength, boldness. Campaign tagline: 'Make in India' with the lion symbol on all promotions.

questions.question: India's 'Sovereign Green Bonds' were first issued in:
options: January 2023 — Rs 16,000 crore raised in two tranches — proceeds used for green infrastructure, renewable energy, clean transport — India's first sovereign green bonds
questions.explanation: India's Sovereign Green Bonds (SGBs — not to be confused with Sovereign Gold Bonds): first issued January 2023. Framework: proceeds for eligible green expenditures — renewable energy (solar, wind), green buildings, clean transport, water, biodiversity. Rs 8,000 crore in two tranches. 10-year and 5-year tenure. Part of India's commitment to achieving net-zero by 2070.

questions.question: 'Cooperative federalism' in India refers to:
options: Centre and States working together collaboratively — GST Council, Finance Commission, NITI Aayog's Team India Hub, joint schemes (Centrally Sponsored Schemes) represent cooperative elements
questions.explanation: Cooperative Federalism: Centre-State partnership and cooperation. Examples: GST Council (Centre+States jointly decide tax rates), Finance Commission (balances Centre-State fiscal needs), NITI Aayog (PM+CMs meet as Governing Council), CSS (Centrally Sponsored Schemes: Centre+State funding). Competitive federalism: states competing for investment ('ease of doing business' rankings).

questions.question: In India's federal structure, 'Concurrent List' (List III, 7th Schedule) includes:
options: Subjects where both Centre and State can legislate — education, forests, family planning, labour, trade unions, price control, bankruptcy, drugs, courts, population control — Centre's law prevails in conflict
Subjects only Centre can legislate (Union List — 97 items)
Subjects only States can legislate (State List — 66 items)
questions.explanation: Concurrent List (52 subjects): both Parliament and State Legislatures can make laws. In case of conflict: Central law prevails (repugnancy doctrine, Article 254). Examples: education (42nd Amendment moved from State to Concurrent), forests, labour laws, trade unions, social security, price control, commercial and industrial monopolies. States have more practical importance in Concurrent subjects.

questions.question: The National Infrastructure and Investment Fund (NIIF) is different from NIP because:
options: NIIF is a fund (invests equity/quasi-equity in infrastructure companies); NIP is a pipeline/plan (list of projects for government and private investment); NIIF has sovereign wealth fund characteristics while NIP is a planning document
NIIF is private, NIP is public
questions.explanation: NIIF (National Infrastructure and Investment Fund, 2015): an institution — invests in infrastructure through three funds (Master, Fund of Funds, Strategic Opportunities). Sovereign wealth fund characteristics with 49% govt ownership. NIP (National Infrastructure Pipeline, 2019): a planning document listing 6,835+ infrastructure projects worth Rs 111 lakh crore for 2019-25. Two distinct instruments.

questions.question: Open Acreage Licensing Policy (OALP) in India is related to:
options: Oil and gas exploration — allows companies to select areas for exploration from National Data Repository and apply for exploration; part of HELP (Hydrocarbon Exploration and Licensing Policy 2016)
questions.explanation: HELP (Hydrocarbon Exploration and Licensing Policy, 2016) replaced NELP (New Exploration and Licensing Policy). Features: revenue sharing model (replaces production sharing contracts), uniform licensing (oil, gas, CBM, shale in one licence), OALP (Open Acreage Licensing — companies choose any available block from data repository). Aims to boost domestic oil/gas production.

questions.question: India's first 'Social Stock Exchange' (SSE) was launched by:
options: BSE and NSE (both) — SEBI framework 2021; allows social enterprises (NGOs, NPOs, and for-profit social enterprises) to raise funds from investors transparently; launched 2023
questions.explanation: Social Stock Exchange (SSE, SEBI framework 2021): separate segment of existing stock exchanges (BSE, NSE) for social sector organizations. Social enterprises can list and raise funds from investors who want social impact alongside financial returns. Separate reporting: Social Impact Metrics. SEBI also introduced 'Zero Coupon Zero Premium' instruments for nonprofits to raise grants through SSE.

questions.question: India's current account in BoP became a surplus briefly in which year?
options: 2020-21 (April-September, COVID quarters) — unprecedented surplus due to collapse of oil imports (lockdown, travel), surge in IT services demand, decline in gold imports, and massive remittances
questions.explanation: India typically runs current account deficit. Exception: 2020-21 Q1 (Apr-Jun): surplus of $19.1 billion (unprecedented) due to: COVID lockdowns → oil import collapse, gold import collapse; while IT service exports boomed (work from home), remittances remained strong. As economy reopened and imports recovered, deficit returned in 2021-22.

questions.question: The concept of 'mixed economy' in India post-1991 means:
options: Market forces determine most resource allocation; government intervenes in: externalities, public goods, infrastructure, strategic sectors, social sector — less government control than pre-1991 but more than pure laissez-faire
questions.explanation: Post-1991 India: hybrid economy — most industries open to private sector with market-based pricing, but government retains: defence production (DRDO, HAL), atomic energy, nuclear power, railways (mostly), strategic minerals, coal. Also intervenes: agricultural prices (MSP), petroleum prices (partially regulated), utilities (electricity distribution tariff regulation), social sector (healthcare, education). 'Market-friendly' rather than 'market-only.'

questions.question: The 'SDG India Index' is published by:
options: NITI Aayog — annual index ranking states and UTs on Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) — 17 goals, 115 indicators; goal: ensure all SDGs achieved by 2030 at state level
questions.explanation: SDG India Index (NITI Aayog, first edition 2018-19): quantifies state/UT progress on SDGs. States ranked: Frontrunner (65-100), Performer (50-64), Aspirant (0-49). Indicators cover: no poverty, zero hunger, good health, quality education, gender equality, clean water, energy, economic growth, reduced inequality, sustainable cities, climate action, etc. Kerala, Himachal Pradesh, Tamil Nadu consistently top performers.

questions.question: India's 'National Mission on Transformative Mobility and Battery Storage' (NMTMBS) aims at:
options: Accelerating adoption of electric vehicles (EVs) — policies for EV manufacturing, charging infrastructure, battery recycling; FAME-II scheme for EV subsidies; targets: 30% EV penetration by 2030 for private cars
questions.explanation: EV Mission: FAME India (Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles): Phase-I (2015-19), Phase-II (2019-24, Rs 10,000 crore): subsidies for EV purchase, charging infrastructure. PLI for Advanced Chemistry Cell batteries (ACC-PLI, Rs 18,100 crore). NMTMBS: inter-ministerial mission for EV ecosystem. India wants 30% EV by 2030 for all categories; 100% EV for 2-wheelers and 3-wheelers.

questions.question: India's 'Smart Cities Mission' outcome metric focuses on:
options: Quality of life improvement for citizens through smart solutions — smart water, transport, energy management, waste management, ICT-based governance, safety — 100 selected cities; Rs 48,000 crore outlay
Only economic growth in selected cities
questions.explanation: Smart Cities Mission (June 2015): 100 cities selected via competition ('City Challenge'). Core infrastructure: smart supply of water, assured electricity, sanitation, solid waste management, efficient urban mobility, affordable housing, IT connectivity, e-governance, safety. Area-Based Development (retrofit, redevelop, greenfield) + Pan-City solutions (ICT). Central grant: Rs 500 crore/city (5 years).

questions.question: Who introduced the concept of Absolute Advantage in international trade?
questions.explanation: Adam Smith in 'The Wealth of Nations' (1776) introduced Absolute Advantage — a country should export goods it can produce more efficiently (absolutely) than others.

questions.question: The Heckscher-Ohlin theorem states that a country will export goods that are intensive in its:
questions.explanation: The H-O theorem: a country exports goods that intensively use its abundant factors. E.g., labour-abundant India exports labour-intensive goods.

questions.question: The Leontief Paradox showed that the USA, a capital-abundant nation, exported:
questions.explanation: Wassily Leontief (1953) found the USA paradoxically exported labour-intensive goods, contradicting the H-O theorem. This is called the Leontief Paradox.

questions.question: Currency depreciation differs from devaluation in that depreciation is:
questions.explanation: Depreciation = market-driven fall in currency value (floating exchange rates). Devaluation = government-decreed reduction under fixed/pegged exchange rate system.

questions.question: Purchasing Power Parity (PPP) theory suggests that exchange rates should equalize:
questions.explanation: PPP: exchange rates adjust so that identical goods cost the same across countries. The 'Big Mac Index' by The Economist is a popular PPP measure.

questions.question: The Consolidated Fund of India is governed by which Article of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Article 266 establishes the Consolidated Fund of India. All revenues, loans raised, and loan repayments received by the Government of India go into this fund.

questions.question: The Contingency Fund of India is kept at the disposal of:
questions.explanation: The Contingency Fund of India (Article 267) is at the President's disposal for urgent unforeseen expenditures. Its size is Rs 500 crore; Parliament must ratify later.

questions.question: The credit multiplier (money multiplier) is the reciprocal of:
questions.explanation: Credit Multiplier = 1/CRR. If CRR = 4%, multiplier = 25. Every Rs 1 of base money creates Rs 25 of deposits. Higher CRR → lower multiplier → less credit creation.

questions.question: W.W. Rostow's 'Stages of Economic Growth' identifies how many stages of development?
questions.explanation: Rostow (1960): 5 stages — (1) Traditional Society, (2) Pre-conditions for Take-off, (3) Take-off, (4) Drive to Maturity, (5) Age of High Mass Consumption.

questions.question: The Harrod-Domar model of economic growth emphasizes the role of:
questions.explanation: Harrod-Domar: Growth Rate = Savings Rate / Capital-Output Ratio. Higher savings → higher investment → higher growth. Used to justify foreign aid (filling 'savings gap') for developing countries.

questions.question: Human Capital Theory, associated with Gary Becker and Theodore Schultz, argues that:
options: Physical capital is the only driver of growth
Investment in education and health raises productivity like investment in machines
Trade is the engine of growth
questions.explanation: Human Capital Theory: education, health, training are investments that increase individual productivity and economic growth. Schultz won Nobel 1979; Becker won Nobel 1992.

questions.question: The Code on Wages, 2019 subsumes how many existing labour laws?
questions.explanation: Code on Wages 2019 subsumes 4 laws: Payment of Wages Act 1936, Minimum Wages Act 1948, Payment of Bonus Act 1965, and Equal Remuneration Act 1976.

questions.question: The Labour Force Participation Rate (LFPR) measures:
options: Percentage of working-age population that is employed
Percentage of population employed in formal sector
Percentage of working-age population that is employed or actively seeking employment
Percentage of population below poverty line
questions.explanation: LFPR = (Labour Force / Working-age Population) × 100. Labour Force = Employed + Unemployed (those seeking jobs). India's female LFPR is among the lowest globally (~25%).

questions.question: Consumer Surplus is the difference between:
options: What consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay
questions.explanation: Consumer Surplus = Willingness to Pay − Market Price. It represents the benefit consumers gain from buying at the market price, which is lower than their maximum willingness to pay.

questions.question: A Giffen good is one where demand increases as price increases because:
options: It has high brand value (Veblen effect)
It is an inferior good where the income effect dominates the substitution effect
questions.explanation: Giffen goods (Robert Giffen): inferior goods where price rise reduces real income so severely that consumers buy more (cannot afford the superior substitute). Classic example: bread in 19th-century England.

questions.question: A Veblen good is characterized by:
options: Demand increasing as price rises due to conspicuous consumption
questions.explanation: Veblen goods (Thorstein Veblen): luxury goods where higher price signals higher status, increasing demand (diamonds, luxury cars, designer goods). Upward-sloping demand curve.

questions.question: A price floor is effective (binding) only when it is set:
questions.explanation: Price floor (minimum price) is binding only above equilibrium price, creating a surplus (supply > demand). E.g., MSP set above market price creates surplus procurement. Below equilibrium = non-binding.

questions.question: Dadabhai Naoroji's 'Drain Theory' argued that British rule caused economic drain through:
options: Forced export of raw materials and remittance of profits/salaries to Britain without equivalent return
questions.explanation: Naoroji (1867): calculated India's 'drain' — export surplus not returned as imports but as remittances to Britain (Home Charges, profit repatriation). Estimated drain as ~£15-30 million/year.

questions.question: Foreign Portfolio Investment (FPI) differs from Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) because FPI:
options: Does not involve management control — it's passive investment in stocks/bonds
Is restricted to government securities only
questions.explanation: FPI: passive investment in financial instruments (stocks, bonds) without management control. FDI: significant ownership/management control (≥10% equity per OECD definition). FPI is more volatile (hot money) vs FDI (more stable).

questions.question: The National Food Security Act (NFSA) 2013 provides subsidized food grains to what percentage of India's population?
questions.explanation: NFSA 2013: covers up to 75% rural + 50% urban population = ~67% of total population (~81.35 crore people). Provides 5 kg/person/month at subsidized rates: Rice Rs 3, Wheat Rs 2, Coarse grains Rs 1/kg.

questions.question: The concept of 'Demographic Dividend' for India refers to the economic benefit arising from:
options: Large elderly population needing care industry
Large working-age population relative to dependents (young + old)
questions.explanation: Demographic Dividend: large working-age population (15-64) relative to dependents boosts savings, investment, and growth. India's window: ~2020-2040. Median age ~28 years. Requires jobs and skill development.

questions.question: Demonetization in India (November 8, 2016) scrapped currency notes of which denominations?
questions.explanation: Demonetization (November 8, 2016): PM Modi announced scrapping of Rs 500 and Rs 1000 notes (86% of currency in circulation). New Rs 500 and Rs 2000 notes introduced. Aims: counter black money, counterfeit, terror financing.

questions.question: The concept of 'Creative Destruction' associated with Joseph Schumpeter describes:
options: Innovation that destroys existing industries while creating new ones, driving capitalist progress
questions.explanation: Schumpeter's Creative Destruction: innovation (new products, methods, markets) makes existing industries obsolete, destroying old capital while creating new. Key driver of long-run capitalist dynamism. (E.g., smartphones destroyed film cameras.)

questions.question: The 'Big Bang' approach to economic reforms (as opposed to 'gradualism') involves:
options: Rapid, simultaneous implementation of multiple major reforms
questions.explanation: Big Bang reforms: comprehensive, rapid liberalization across sectors simultaneously (Poland 1990, Russia early 1990s). Contrasts with China's and India's gradual approach. India's 1991 reforms were bold but not full 'big bang.'

questions.question: The concept of 'Opportunity Cost' is best illustrated by:
options: A student choosing to study instead of working (foregone salary = opportunity cost of education)
A tax paid to the government
questions.explanation: Opportunity Cost: value of the next best alternative foregone when making a choice. Example: studying full-time costs the salary you could have earned. It's implicit (not a cash payment) but real economic cost.

questions.question: India's Planning Commission was replaced by NITI Aayog in January 2015 with a change in focus from:
options: Central planning with resource allocation to policy advisory and competitive federalism
Five year plans to annual plans
questions.explanation: Planning Commission (1950-2014): top-down central planning, resource allocation to states. NITI Aayog (Jan 1, 2015): policy think-tank, cooperative federalism, no fund-allocation power. PM is its chairman.

questions.question: Which index measures the inequality of income or wealth distribution in a country?
questions.explanation: Gini Coefficient (Corrado Gini, 1912): 0 = perfect equality; 1 = maximum inequality. Derived from Lorenz Curve. India's Gini ~0.36 (income). Used alongside HDI to measure inclusive development.

questions.question: What is the role of the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) of India in public finance?
options: Audit all government expenditures and receipts to ensure accountability
questions.explanation: CAG (Article 148): supreme audit institution. Audits accounts of Union and State governments, PSUs. Reports tabled in Parliament (Union) and State Legislatures. Independent of executive; reports to President.

questions.question: The Kuznets Curve (Environmental Kuznets Curve) in economics hypothesizes that environmental degradation:
options: First increases then decreases as per-capita income rises (inverted U-shape)
questions.explanation: EKC: at low income levels, growth increases pollution. Beyond a threshold, cleaner technologies and stricter regulations reduce pollution. Named after Simon Kuznets (who proposed income-inequality inverted-U, not environment).

questions.question: India's National Pension System (NPS) is regulated by:
options: Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority (PFRDA)
questions.explanation: NPS regulated by PFRDA (Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority), established under PFRDA Act 2013. NPS launched 2004 for government employees (replaced old pension). Extended to all citizens 2009.

questions.question: India's Sovereign Wealth Fund equivalent is:
questions.explanation: NIIF (2015): India's quasi-sovereign wealth fund. Government of India owns 49%, remaining 51% from institutional investors (ADIA of Abu Dhabi, CDPQ of Canada, etc.). Invests in infrastructure assets.

questions.question: The Marginal Propensity to Save (MPS) + Marginal Propensity to Consume (MPC) always equals:
questions.explanation: MPC + MPS = 1 (an additional unit of income is either consumed or saved, nothing else). Investment Multiplier k = 1/(1−MPC) = 1/MPS. Higher MPC → higher multiplier effect of government spending.

questions.question: The 'Smile Curve' concept in international trade (coined by Stan Shih of Acer) shows that:
options: Profits are highest in the middle of the value chain (manufacturing)
Profits are highest at both ends of the value chain (R&D/design and marketing/services), not in manufacturing
Developing countries benefit most from trade
questions.explanation: Smile Curve: value added is highest at R&D/design (upstream) and brand/marketing/services (downstream) ends; manufacturing in the middle captures less value. Explains why Apple earns more than Foxconn despite Foxconn making iPhones.

questions.question: India's National Monetization Pipeline (NMP) aims to monetize government/PSU brownfield assets worth approximately:
questions.explanation: NMP (August 2021): Rs 6 lakh crore worth of brownfield infrastructure assets to be monetized over 4 years (FY2022-25). Assets: NHAI roads, railway stations, airports, power lines, gas pipelines, telecom towers. NITI Aayog designed it.

questions.question: India's longest Five Year Plan period — which plan ran for 7 years?
options: Annual Plans (1966-69) filled the gap after 3rd FYP
questions.explanation: 3rd FYP (1961-66) was followed by Annual Plans (1966-69) — a 'plan holiday' due to 1965 Indo-Pak War and drought. Then 5th FYP (1974-78) was terminated early by Janata government (1978). 6th FYP: two versions!

questions.question: Privatization means:
options: Transfer of ownership or management of state-owned enterprises to private sector — through disinvestment (selling government equity), strategic sale, or outright sale
questions.explanation: Privatization: government ownership/control transferred to private sector. Disinvestment: government sells part of its equity in PSUs (Public Sector Undertakings). Strategic sale: transfer of management control + equity. Full privatization: 100% sale. India's disinvestment target: Rs 65,000 crore (2023-24). Air India sold to Tata Sons (2022) is a major strategic disinvestment.

questions.question: IRDAI (Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India) was established to:
options: Regulate and develop the insurance industry in India — established 1999, HQ Hyderabad; oversees life insurance (LIC), general insurance, and health insurance companies
questions.explanation: IRDAI (1999, under IRDA Act 1999): insurance sector regulator. Regulates: LIC (Life Insurance Corporation), private life insurance companies, general insurance (motor, health, fire, marine), and reinsurance (GIC Re). Insurance sector opened to private players in 2000. FDI in insurance: increased to 74% in 2021. HQ: Hyderabad.

questions.question: India's Demographic Dividend refers to:
options: Economic growth potential from having a large working-age population (15-64) compared to dependents — India's median age ~28 years; window: 2020-2050 approx.
questions.explanation: Demographic Dividend: when a country has more working-age people than dependents (young+old), it can boost savings, investment, and economic growth. India's dependency ratio is favorable in 2020s-2040s. To harness it: skills development, education, job creation, and health improvements are critical. East Asian economic miracle largely attributed to demographic dividend.

questions.question: The concept of 'trickle-up economics' (bottom-up growth) argues:
options: Investing in the poor (through education, health, social security) increases demand, improves productivity, and generates broader economic growth — more effective than trickle-down
questions.explanation: Bottom-up/inclusive growth: investing in the poor directly (education, health, social protection) → higher productivity, greater consumer demand, reduced inequality → sustainable growth. Evidence from Kerala model, Nordic countries. Contrast with trickle-down (top-first approach). Linked to Amartya Sen's capabilities approach and UNDP's HDI concept.

questions.question: The National Monetization Pipeline (NMP) was announced by:
options: FM Nirmala Sitharaman in August 2021 — plan to monetize Rs 6 lakh crore of brownfield government infrastructure assets (roads, railways, power lines, gas pipelines, airports) over 4 years
questions.explanation: NMP (National Monetization Pipeline, 2021-2025): aims to raise Rs 6 lakh crore by transferring operation of existing government assets (brownfield) to private sector for a fixed period (not selling assets). Assets: NHAI roads, railway stations/freight terminals, power lines (PGCIL), gas pipelines (GAIL), airports (AAI), ports. Asset monetization ≠ privatization.

questions.question: The National Health Mission (NHM) ASHA workers are:
options: Accredited Social Health Activists — community health workers (one per 1000 rural population) who serve as link between community and health facilities; mobilize people for health services
questions.explanation: ASHA (Accredited Social Health Activist): female community health worker (Class 8 educated, 25+ years, married/widowed/divorced). Functions: promote institutional deliveries, immunization, family planning, TB treatment adherence; accompany patients to health facilities; receive performance-based incentives. About 10 lakh ASHAs in India.

questions.question: The concept of 'Comparative Advantage' in international trade was developed by:
options: David Ricardo (1817, 'On the Principles of Political Economy and Taxation') — countries should specialize in producing goods they produce relatively more efficiently, even if less efficient absolutely
questions.explanation: Comparative Advantage (Ricardo): a country should specialize in goods where it has the lowest opportunity cost relative to other goods, not necessarily the lowest absolute cost. Even if one country is more efficient in all goods, both benefit from trade through specialization. Basis for international free trade theory. Example: India in IT services, China in manufacturing.

questions.question: The Lok Adalat system in India resolves disputes by:
options: Conciliation and compromise between parties — decisions have the force of court decree, no court fees, no appeal possible (binding); organized by State Legal Services Authority under Legal Services Authorities Act 1987
questions.explanation: Lok Adalat (People's Court): alternative dispute resolution forum. Cases are settled by mutual agreement facilitated by a panel (retired judge + lawyers). Benefits: fast (one day), free (no fees), final (no appeal; if no settlement, returns to court). Covers: motor accident claims, labour disputes, matrimonial cases (except divorce), land disputes. Annual disposal: crores of cases.

questions.question: The Employees' Provident Fund Organisation (EPFO) manages:
options: Provident Fund (12% + 12% from employee+employer), Employees' Pension Scheme (EPS), and Employees' Deposit Linked Insurance (EDLI) for organized sector workers (threshold: 20+ employees)
questions.explanation: EPFO (established 1952, EPF Act 1952): statutory body under Ministry of Labour. Manages: EPF (both contribute 12% of basic+DA; employee's 12% → EPF, employer's 12% → 8.33% EPS + 3.67% EPF), EPS (pension of up to Rs 7,500/month), EDLI (life insurance of up to Rs 7 lakh on death in service). Covers establishments with 20+ employees.

questions.question: The concept of 'creative destruction' in economics was given by:
options: Joseph Schumpeter (1942, 'Capitalism, Socialism and Democracy') — innovation destroys existing markets/firms and creates new ones; driving force of capitalism; e.g., internet destroyed newspapers, smartphones killed Nokia
questions.explanation: Creative Destruction (Schumpeter): capitalism's engine of growth is innovation. New technologies, products, and firms continuously destroy old ones — enabling higher productivity and better products. Example: automobiles destroyed horse carriages, internet destroyed physical retail (Amazon vs Sears), smartphones destroyed camera and music player industries. Short-term disruption, long-term growth.

questions.question: India's 'Ease of Living' index measures:
options: Quality of life in Indian cities — covers governance (administrative, social, tech), social (education, health, identity), economic (employment, economy), and physical (waste, water, housing, mobility) pillars
Same as Ease of Doing Business
questions.explanation: Ease of Living Index (Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs, 2018, updated 2020): assesses quality of life in Indian cities (111 cities). Four pillars: Governance (20%), Social (30%), Economic (20%), Physical (30%). Assessed at: services (58%) and outcomes (42%). Also: Municipal Performance Index (MPI) for city governance. Bengaluru, Pune, Ahmedabad, Chennai have topped rankings.

questions.question: The 'Bharat Stage' emission standards for vehicles (BS-VI since 2020) are India's equivalent of:
options: Euro emission standards (Euro-VI) — India leapfrogged from BS-IV to BS-VI in one step (skipping BS-V) in April 2020; requires CNG/petrol vehicles to reduce NOx, PM by 80%+ from BS-IV
questions.explanation: Bharat Stage (BS) emission standards: based on European emission norms. India leapfrogged BS-V and went directly from BS-IV to BS-VI (April 1, 2020 — ahead of many countries). BS-VI equivalent to Euro-VI: drastic reduction in particulate matter (PM) and oxides of nitrogen (NOx). Required oil companies to supply BS-VI fuel, vehicle manufacturers to retrofit engines.

questions.question: The NITI Aayog's 'Aspirational Districts Programme' targets:
options: 112 most underdeveloped districts (selected based on composite index of health, nutrition, education, agriculture, financial inclusion, infrastructure, skill development) to rapidly improve outcomes
questions.explanation: Aspirational Districts Programme (2018): 112 districts ranked on 49 indicators across 5 themes: Health & Nutrition, Education, Agriculture & Water Resources, Financial Inclusion & Skill Development, Basic Infrastructure. Real-time monitoring on Championing Change portal. 'Competitive and cooperative federalism' — districts compete and learn from each other. Success: significant improvement in health, nutrition, education indicators.

questions.question: The Sagarmala Programme focuses on:
options: Port-led development, coastal shipping, port connectivity
